On November 7, 1940,
the Tacoma Narrows
Bridge
collapsed into Puget
Sound. It had opened on
July 1. Four months
later, it was gone.
The wind that morning
was not extraordinary.
It was measured at 38
miles per hour at 7:30
a.m.
and about 42 miles per
hour near the bridge's
east end two hours
later. The bridge had
already ridden through
earlier
autumn storms with
winds over 50 miles
per
hour. What it could not
survive was the
way its own motion
changed the air around
it, and what that air
then did back
to the bridge. For a
little over an hour
before the collapse, the
bridge twisted.
Not simply swayed, and
not only bounced.
The roadway rotated
along its length, one
side rising as the other
fell, in a motion that
grew steadily larger.
Leonard Coatsworth,
whose car had been
thrown against
the curb, climbed out
and crawled toward the
East Tower as the
deck rolled beneath
him. Engineers and
photographers were
watching.
Motion-picture footage
survives.
The bridge did not
fail because November
7 brought some
unprecedented
gale. It failed because
the bridge and the wind
entered a feedback
cycle the design had
never been tested
against.
Once that cycle took
hold, each oscillation
helped feed the next.
This episode is not
about a freak storm
or a cartoon version of
bad engineering.
It is about what
happens when a
structure is optimized
for the forces
engineers know how
to calculate, while a
different class of
forces
remains outside the
design process.
The original proposal
for a bridge across the
Narrows came from
Clark Eldridge,
lead engineer for the
Washington State
Highway Department.
His design was
conservative by the
standards of the day: a
two-lane suspension
bridge with deep, open
stiffening trusses,
twenty-five feet deep,
along the deck.
That design was not
the one that got built.
When Washington
sought federal funding,
the Public Works
Administration required
outside
consultants. Leon
Moisseiff, one of the
most prominent
suspension bridge
engineers in the United
States,
redesigned the
superstructure. He had
helped shape the
theory behind some of
the country's great
suspension bridges. He
was respected and
accomplished.
He also told the PWA
his revisions would cut
the estimated cost
from roughly eleven
million
dollars to seven. The
critical change was the
deck. Instead of
Eldridge's deep open
trusses, Moisseiff used
solid plate girders just
eight feet
deep. Where Eldridge's
deck would have let
much
more air pass through,
Moisseiff's presented a
broader
solid surface to the
wind.
The bridge was also
extraordinarily slender.
Its center span was
2,800 feet long,
while the deck was
only 39 feet wide
between the cables, a
ratio of one to
seventy-two. For bridge
designers of the 1930s,
that kind of lightness
and grace looked like
the future.
The redesign was
approved. Construction
began in November
1938. The bridge opened
to traffic on July 1,
1940.
Almost immediately, it
moved.
Drivers and pedestrians
noticed the vertical
undulation first. The
deck rose and fell in
long waves. The motion
was so conspicuous
that
the bridge quickly
acquired a nickname:
Galloping Gertie.
Engineers knew about
the movement. Before
the collapse, the state
had already hired
University of
Washington engineer F.
B. Farquharson to
study the oscillations
and recommend
remedies. Tie-down
cables had been
installed on the
side spans. Wind
deflectors were under
active
consideration. The
movement was not
treated as an
immediate emergency.
It was treated as an
alarming
peculiarity, but still a
manageable one.
People drove across
the bridge for the
experience.
Each day the bridge
moved and remained
standing
made the motion easier
to categorize as a
property of the bridge
rather than evidence of
a failure already in
progress.
That matters. Visible
anomaly had been
normalized
before the catastrophic
mode ever arrived.
On the morning of
November 7, the
bridge's behavior
changed.
The familiar vertical
motion gave way to
torsional motion.
Shortly after 10:00 a.m.,
the deck began
twisting. By 11:10 a.m.,
the center span had
fallen.
What followed was not
the wind simply
overpowering
the structure. It was a
flexible structure
interacting
with wind in a way the
design framework of
the time did not know
how to
evaluate well. To
understand why,
you have to look at five
conditions that
were in place before
the first cable was
spun.
First: the redesign
prioritized slenderness,
economy, and visual
grace.
Moisseiff's work grew
out of deflection
theory,
an advanced framework
for suspension bridges.
In that framework, the
dead weight of the
bridge, the cable
system, and the
stiffness
already built into the
structure could do far
more
of the stabilizing work
than older practice had
assumed. The payoff
was real. Bridges could
be longer, lighter,
cheaper, and in the
eyes of many
engineers,
more beautiful. For the
loads that theory
modeled
well, it was powerful.
Tacoma Narrows was
not built by ignoring
engineering.
It was built by applying
advanced engineering
within the limits of
what that engineering
had
formalized. Second:
bridge design theory
still treated wind
mainly as a static load,
not a dynamic
conversation
between air and
structure. In the 1930s,
suspension bridge
engineers were
highly capable at
analyzing gravity loads,
traffic loads, and
steady horizontal wind
pressure.
What they did not yet
have was a mature,
standardized
framework for
aerodynamic
behavior in very
flexible long-span
bridges.
Wind was generally
treated as a force that
pushed on the
structure, not as
something that changed
when the structure
itself began to move.
That distinction is
everything. If a bridge
moves in wind,
it changes the airflow
around its deck.
The changed airflow
alters the force on the
deck. That altered
force changes the next
motion.
The bridge is no longer
just resisting wind.
It is helping create the
conditions of its own
next displacement.
Dynamic wind-tunnel
testing
existed in aeronautics.
It was not yet a
routine requirement in
suspension-bridge
design.
Third: the bridge's
motion had already
been absorbed
into the category of
spectacle. Between
July and November
1940,
the bridge's oscillations
were visible,
documented, and widely
discussed.
Farquharson was
studying them.
Remedies were being
developed. But traffic
continued, the
nickname stuck,
and the bridge's motion
became part of its
identity in public life.
When a system
produces conspicuous
abnormal behavior
and survives, people
begin to discount the
warning.
The bridge taught its
observers that
movement could be
dramatic without being
fatal.
That lesson held until it
didn't.
Fourth: the design had
not been subjected to
the kind of
aerodynamic testing
that later became
standard. This was the
clearest gap.
The problem was not
that the calculations
were sloppy.
The problem was that
the design process of
1940 did not yet
routinely demand a
dynamic aerodynamic
investigation of a
bridge deck
like this one. After the
collapse,
the federal
investigating board
identified excessive
flexibility, the
aerodynamic effect of
the solid
plate-girder deck, and
the need for
wind-tunnel model
testing as central
lessons. Those lessons
were learned after the
bridge fell,
not before it was built.
Fifth: once the bridge
entered large torsional
motion,
the motion itself began
feeding the forces
acting on it. Engineers
still debate some
details of
the transition from
vertical oscillation to
catastrophic twisting.
But the standard
modern
explanation centers on
self-excited torsional
flutter,
a self-reinforcing
twisting instability.
In that kind of
instability, the twisting
of the deck changes the
airflow in a way that
adds energy back into
the twist. The motion
becomes
self-reinforcing.
The wind did not need
to become a hurricane.
It only needed to keep
supplying the flow field
that the moving bridge
was already
turning into fuel. This
is why Tacoma
Narrows
is so often
misunderstood.
The famous footage
tempts people to call it
a simple resonance
story, as if the wind
just happened to hit
one magic frequency
and
the bridge obediently
shook itself apart.
That is too simple. The
stronger account is an
aerodynamic one:
vertical motion, vortex
effects, torsional
instability, and a bridge
deck so flexible that
once the twisting mode
took hold, the airflow
and the structure began
driving each
other. The bridge did
not merely fail in the
wind.
It became
aerodynamically
unstable in the wind.
The collapse had an
immediate effect on
the profession, even if
the exact mechanism
was
not settled in a single
afternoon.
Investigations began at
once.
The federal board
reported in March 1941.
University of
Washington wind-tunnel
work continued through
the 1940s. The Advisory
Board on the
Investigation of
Suspension Bridges
met from 1942 to
1954.
Engineers re-evaluated
other long-span
suspension bridges and
began designing later
spans
with far greater
attention to
aerodynamic stability.
The replacement
Tacoma Narrows
Bridge, opened in 1950,
looked much more like
Eldridge's
original instinct than
Moisseiff's 1940 deck.
It used a much deeper
open stiffening truss,
features to let air pass
through the deck,
and additional damping
devices.
That was not an
aesthetic correction. It
was a new engineering
answer to a newly
formalized problem.
The collapse did not
reveal engineering
negligence in the
simple moralized
sense. Moisseiff was
not guessing. He was
applying the best
accepted theory
available to him, and
that theory had worked
impressively
within its domain. What
the collapse revealed
was
that the domain itself
had an edge.
Past that edge, the
equations were not
wrong. They were
incomplete. The deeper
failure was not
that the bridge was
slender, or beautiful, or
inexpensive.
It was that the design
tools used to judge the
bridge were optimized
for loads they
had been built to
handle, while
aerodynamic instability
in flexible bridge
decks
had not yet been fully
absorbed into ordinary
bridge practice. The
bridge was not
dynamically vetted
against that problem in
the way later
generations would
consider essential.
This kind of failure
recurs across the
history of engineering.
It is not the failure of
knowingly ignoring
a risk. It is the failure
of working inside a
framework that has not
yet formalized a real
risk, even when clues
to that
risk are already
present.
In aviation,
aerodynamic behavior
was already
central.
In suspension-bridge
design, it had not yet
crossed
fully into routine design
practice.
The knowledge was
nearby. The discipline
boundary was real.
After November 7, 1940,
that boundary did not
survive intact. The
field changed because
it had
to. Wind-tunnel testing
became commonplace.
Aerodynamic stability
became a standard
concern in long-span
bridge design.
Designers paid much
closer
attention to deck
shape, torsional
stiffness,
damping, and the way
moving structures feed
energy
back into the air around
them. That is how
engineering learns.
Not cleanly. Not
abstractly. Often by
discovering, in the
wreckage of a
real structure, the
exact point where a
trusted model stopped
matching the world.
The Tacoma Narrows
Bridge twisted for a
little over an hour
before it fell.
Cameras were running.
Engineers were
watching.
By then, the bridge was
already doing
something
its designers had never
been required to prove
it could survive.
Everything needed for
the failure
was present long
before November 7:
the shallow plate-girder
deck, the extraordinary
slenderness, the weak
torsional resistance,
the absence of dynamic
aerodynamic testing,
and a profession that
had not yet made those
questions routine.
The wind that morning
did not create the
design weakness.
It revealed it. What
failed at Tacoma
Narrows
was a bridge. What else
failed was the
boundary of a model.
The failure was real.
The cause chain was
longer.
Next time: another
design failure.
Another system that
taught itself to ignore
what
it had not yet learned
how to name.

theory,
an advanced framework
for suspension bridges.
In that framework, the
dead weight of the
bridge, the cable
system, and the
stiffness
already built into the
structure could do far
more
of the stabilizing work
than older practice had
assumed. The payoff
was real. Bridges could
be longer, lighter,
cheaper, and in the
eyes of many
engineers,
more beautiful. For the
loads that theory
modeled
well, it was powerful.
Tacoma Narrows was
not built by ignoring
engineering.
It was built by applying
advanced engineering
within the limits of
what that engineering
had
formalized. Second:
bridge design theory
still treated wind
mainly as a static load,
not a dynamic
conversation
between air and
structure. In the 1930s,
suspension bridge
engineers were
highly capable at
analyzing gravity loads,
traffic loads, and
steady horizontal wind
pressure.
What they did not yet
have was a mature,
standardized
framework for
aerodynamic
behavior in very
flexible long-span
bridges.
Wind was generally
treated as a force that
pushed on the
structure, not as
something that changed
when the structure
itself began to move.
That distinction is
everything. If a bridge
moves in wind,
it changes the airflow
around its deck.
The changed airflow
alters the force on the
deck. That altered
force changes the next
motion.
The bridge is no longer
just resisting wind.
It is helping create the
conditions of its own
next displacement.
Dynamic wind-tunnel
testing
existed in aeronautics.
It was not yet a
routine requirement in
suspension-bridge
design.
Third: the bridge's
motion had already
been absorbed
into the category of
spectacle. Between
July and November
1940,
the bridge's oscillations
were visible,
documented, and widely
discussed.
Farquharson was
studying them.
Remedies were being
developed. But traffic
continued, the
nickname stuck,
and the bridge's motion
became part of its
identity in public life.
When a system
produces conspicuous
abnormal behavior
and survives, people
begin to discount the
warning.
The bridge taught its
observers that
movement could be
dramatic without being
fatal.
That lesson held until it
didn't.
Fourth: the design had
not been subjected to
the kind of
aerodynamic testing
that later became
standard. This was the
clearest gap.
The problem was not
that the calculations
were sloppy.
The problem was that
the design process of
1940 did not yet
routinely demand a
dynamic aerodynamic
investigation of a
bridge deck
like this one. After the
collapse,
the federal
investigating board
identified excessive
flexibility, the
aerodynamic effect of
the solid
plate-girder deck, and
the need for
wind-tunnel model
testing as central
lessons. Those lessons
were learned after the
bridge fell,
not before it was built.
Fifth: once the bridge
entered large torsional
motion,
the motion itself began
feeding the forces
acting on it. Engineers
still debate some
details of
the transition from
vertical oscillation to
catastrophic twisting.
But the standard
modern
explanation centers on
self-excited torsional
flutter,
a self-reinforcing
twisting instability.
In that kind of
instability, the twisting
of the deck changes the
airflow in a way that
adds energy back into
the twist. The motion
becomes
self-reinforcing.
The wind did not need
to become a hurricane.
It only needed to keep
supplying the flow field
that the moving bridge
was already
turning into fuel. This
is why Tacoma
Narrows
is so often
misunderstood.
The famous footage
tempts people to call it
a simple resonance
story, as if the wind
just happened to hit
one magic frequency
and
the bridge obediently
shook itself apart.
That is too simple. The
stronger account is an
aerodynamic one:
vertical motion, vortex
effects, torsional
instability, and a bridge
deck so flexible that
once the twisting mode
took hold, the airflow
and the structure began
driving each
other. The bridge did
not merely fail in the
wind.
It became
aerodynamically
unstable in the wind.
The collapse had an
immediate effect on
the profession, even if
the exact mechanism
was
not settled in a single
afternoon.
Investigations began at
once.
The federal board
reported in March 1941.
University of
Washington wind-tunnel
work continued through
the 1940s. The Advisory
Board on the
Investigation of
Suspension Bridges
met from 1942 to
1954.
Engineers re-evaluated
other long-span
suspension bridges and
began designing later
spans
with far greater
attention to
aerodynamic stability.
The replacement
Tacoma Narrows
Bridge, opened in 1950,
looked much more like
Eldridge's
original instinct than
Moisseiff's 1940 deck.
It used a much deeper
open stiffening truss,
features to let air pass
through the deck,
and additional damping
devices.
That was not an
aesthetic correction. It
was a new engineering
answer to a newly
formalized problem.
The collapse did not
reveal engineering
negligence in the
simple moralized
sense. Moisseiff was
not guessing. He was
applying the best
accepted theory
available to him, and
that theory had worked
impressively
within its domain. What
the collapse revealed
was
that the domain itself
had an edge.
Past that edge, the
equations were not
wrong. They were
incomplete. The deeper
failure was not
that the bridge was
slender, or beautiful, or
inexpensive.
It was that the design
tools used to judge the
bridge were optimized
for loads they
had been built to
handle, while
aerodynamic instability
in flexible bridge
decks
had not yet been fully
absorbed into ordinary
bridge practice. The
bridge was not
dynamically vetted
against that problem in
the way later
generations would
consider essential.
This kind of failure
recurs across the
history of engineering.
It is not the failure of
knowingly ignoring
a risk. It is the failure
of working inside a
framework that has not
yet formalized a real
risk, even when clues
to that
risk are already
present.
In aviation,
aerodynamic behavior
was already
central.
In suspension-bridge
design, it had not yet
crossed
fully into routine design
practice.
The knowledge was
nearby. The discipline
boundary was real.
After November 7, 1940,
that boundary did not
survive intact. The
field changed because
it had
to. Wind-tunnel testing
became commonplace.
Aerodynamic stability
became a standard
concern in long-span
bridge design.
Designers paid much
closer
attention to deck
shape, torsional
stiffness,
damping, and the way
moving structures feed
energy
back into the air around
them. That is how
engineering learns.
Not cleanly. Not
abstractly. Often by
discovering, in the
wreckage of a
real structure, the
exact point where a
trusted model stopped
matching the world.
The Tacoma Narrows
Bridge twisted for a
little over an hour
before it fell.
Cameras were running.
Engineers were
watching.
By then, the bridge was
already doing
something
its designers had never
been required to prove
it could survive.
Everything needed for
the failure
was present long
before November 7:
the shallow plate-girder
deck, the extraordinary
slenderness, the weak
torsional resistance,
the absence of dynamic
aerodynamic testing,
and a profession that
had not yet made those
questions routine.
The wind that morning
did not create the
design weakness.
It revealed it. What
failed at Tacoma
Narrows
was a bridge. What else
failed was the
boundary of a model.
The failure was real.
The cause chain was
longer.
Next time: another
design failure.
Another system that
taught itself to ignore
what
it had not yet learned
how to name.
